sentences.text: My name is Sally Huppert, Sally, S-A-L-L-Y H-U-P-P-E-R-T.
At birth is Sara Peller.
Sara Peller, S-A-R-A P-E-L-L-E-R.
Yes, Alla.
A-L-L-A.
In-- I'm born in 1910.
The date, December 6.
Stroza.
S-T-R-O-Z-E.
In Poland.
Yes.
Yes.
It's a small town, and just at the trains was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of trains changed there.
Near Nowa [?
Sarzyna. ?]
Apartment.
My families was-- I was the oldest, Sally.
Now it's Rega, younger one, Necha, Hilda, the youngest, Hilda.
Very close.
Blanka, I forgot Blanka.
Fighting.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
I go in Stroza, and later we moved to Grybow, I went to-- in Grybow in school to cheder.
I went in school at Grybow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all.
I go, go to a public school, yes.
I go to Hebrew school.
Polish.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a girls school.
Yes.
I have friends, very good friends.
No.
Never.
He didn't support.
He, he is-- he came from the army.
And he was in Italy.
He was-- he came sick.
Yes.
I had uncle in Germany.
He support us.
Not so very religious.
Yes.
My house was always a lot of people, a lot of children.
And my girlfriends, they came to us, and they stay with us.
We read.
We learn a lot.
We didn't have no television.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No radio.
Nearby, no.
I used to go every vacation, I used to go to my grandparents in nice-- their-- a large family.
There was a large family, uncles and aunts, and I visit them, just a small town.
Mszana Dolna.
Always the summers with them.
No, my family didn't go with me.
I was just there.
On that farm we visit our aunts.
We go for trips, in neighborhood, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's all, not much.
Yes.
Later, we moved to Rabka.
Oh maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was young.
I don't remember the date.
Rabka was a beautiful town.
Rabka is-- was a lot of people came for this, with children for baths, for massages, for inhalations, and always music.
And I used to love to go to listen to music.
Rabka, was near Zakopane.
Yes.
I have friends.
I have customers.
I had a corset factory.
I-- not factory.
No factory, only corsetiere.
I make girdles and brassieres for the customers what they came to Rabka, and I make money.
Very young.
But I was-- before, I was working in by a lawyer.
I learned to type there.
And I went to the refinery, refinery.
Petroleum refinery.
Refinery.
I was their secretary.
And I took care of all the expediency of all the workers.
I make a lot of money there.
No, in Stroza.
This was not far from Rabka.
I traveled from Grybow to Stroza to work.
Yes.
No, they were very nice to me.
Maybe they've-- because they need me.
Of course.
Of course.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
We were very nervous for it.
We worry about us.
But we couldn't help it.
I remember my uncle came from-- I had two uncles in Germany.
And each have factories.
Stockings, they make stockings.
They colored them.
And they were German, in Germany, with children.
And we find out about.
And they, they went to Swiebodzin.
They send them away from their house.
They were eating supper and the Germans came and sent them away to the [?
Swiebodzin. ?]
To [?
Swiebodzin. ?] And they kill-- and from there they, they want to kill them.
But I remember my uncle, I don't know if they want to kill him.
I don't know.
They were very disappointed at the Germans.
And they came to-- went to Poland, and they stay in Poland.
Yes.
That's right.
Yes, but they couldn't afford it.
No.
Oh, this is a long story and a hard story.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] But I have to tell you.
They came, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] we find out the Gestapo was going to be there in, in this Rabka.
We were very sad, sad about it.
But we couldn't help it.
They took our children, my sisters.
They took to Tereska.
Tereska was a place where they say-- where they-- where they had the headquarters, headquarters.
And all the children, the girls, they have to clean for them.
They sent from the Judenrat.
Because they went to the Judenrat.
They need girls to clean.
And the, the Judenrat sent us.
Yeah.
Yes.
No.
I was not taken, because I was breadwinner.
And I worked for the German girls.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But later they took everything, everybody.
Yes, making-- yes.
They used to come to me.
But I went to-- saw some of [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], that's a woman who take care of the children.
She take care of the children.
So she ask me I should take off the arm band.
I have this.
Because she doesn't want that the children should know.
I did take off-- in there, it was before the holidays.
I went there, and I-- she had a friend, Rosenbaum also her friend.
He came there visiting her.
And I saw him, he saw me.
You know, I was without the arm band.
She said, [NON-ENGLISH].
He said, [NON-ENGLISH] arm band?
And she explained him I-- she asked me about it.
And he was-- he didn't want to listen.
He, he say, throw me out.
And I went out.
And he went after me with the gun.
He wanted to shoot me.
I hide under the stairs.
I don't I didn't-- he didn't see me.
And I was lucky I-- he didn't kill me.
Do you remember when that was?
This was in the beginning.
Yes.
Rosenbaum was the head of the Gestapo.
And he, he killed people.
He-- I must tell you.
It was a family there, and they're Jews.
And they have two children.
And Rosenbaum is-- with the name of Rosenbaum.
And he said they have to be killed.
In a Friday night, he sent for them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They have a boy.
He was working.
And they sent for the boy.
And the boy came home, and they want to take him.
They didn't take him.
The boy asked, should light candles.
And later they came, the Gestapo, and they kill all three of them.
All four of them, the mother, the father and two children.
Because they have the same name what he-- he has, he had.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We find out from the people, what they're working in the Gestapo.
Yes, every day my mother was worrying, every day they working, they, they clean the windows, Gestapo man-- oh, what it his name?
Another one, it was a higher one.
He was the one.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
My father was-- had papers.
He was invalid.
And I bring the papers to the-- to-- what is his name?
I don't remember his name.
Yes, to the head there.
And I tell him he's-- he took the papers.
And he didn't send for my father.
But my father moved away, ran away from Rabka.
He went to his sister to [NON-ENGLISH].
And they killed him there.
They-- all the-- all the Polacks [NON-ENGLISH].
And they say, [NON-ENGLISH], all the Jew they took to Bobowa.
And they killed in Bobowa all the Jews.
They make a mass grave, a mass grave.
In Bobowa.
I don't remember.
I don't know when.
No, the Germans.
I remained in Rabka with my mother and sisters.
My sisters were working in Gestapo.
And I was working also.
And we wait till that one evening, they came, tomorrow, we have to-- people-- my mother has to go.
They killed them all.
So I said to my mother, why you should go away?
We ran away.
All of us ran away from Rabka.
And they were looking for us.
They couldn't find us, because we, we didn't went together.
Everybody went in different direction.
I had a sister.
She's still-- I don't know what happened to her.
She-- they probably killed her.
Necha.
It was my home, but a ghetto.
They close up the street.
That's all.
We have to go out from 3:00 to-- no, from 3:00 to 4:00, or 4:00 to 5:00.
And if we need something we have to go out in this time, just small city.
No, this a-- this was a country.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was beautiful there.
Yeah.
Not nice.
No, no wire.
No wire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We never went away.
From the Gestapo-- no, I don't know from, the Gestapo or from the Judenrat, I don't know.
I don't remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We know about-- they were talking about all the children, hear about this in the Gestapo.
They were working there.
To a forest near, near some boy, who bring us milk, and a girl came-- came just in the same day.
She came, and she said to my mother, you can come in my house.
I live alone.
So my mother came in her house, with my sister, and the, the youngest, Hilda, came her house.
So, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she said that she can stay in her house.
And later, she throw away-- throw out my mother.
Because she find out they are going to kill her, if she's going to keep Jews.
So she throw my mother out.
And I make up with my mother.
I have to pick, I'm going to pick her up, and we're going to go to where all, all the Jews are going.
I wanted to go to Cracow.
Then I'm not-- I didn't know where to look for her.
Since she throw away, she said, she's gone somewhere else.
And I didn't know where to look for her.
And I didn't see her till then, till today.
I know they-- a woman came.
I went to my woman, what she-- I was working for her.
And she was a very good-- a good friend.
And she said, I know your mother was killed.
In the same day, she tell me, your mother was-- your sister was killed, and your mother was killed.
A Polish woman, a good woman.
She came to my house, and she said, Sally, you can stay at my house.
And she keep me in the-- in the attic.
No.
A couple days.
Yes, yeah.
Later I went from her house, I went to Cracow
To Sosnowiec.
Because I have a friend in Warsaw.
And she said when something happen, come to Sosnowiec.
I take you to Warsaw, a Polish girl.
Well, from Rabka, a run by legs through forest.
I came to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to Cracow, I didn't go.
I went to the train.
Now I remember.
I went to the train.
And I don't know how they didn't see me.
But they have black uniforms, the Ukrainians.
No.
No.
I make believe I sleep.
It was a sitting car, old women around me.
So I beg her she let me sleep on their arm.
They didn't wake me.
I didn't speak with no one on the train.
To Sosnowiec.
By train.
And when I came to Sosnowiec, they-- I was very dizzy.
I went to a cafeteria there by the train, and I did wrong.
And the man was nice, and said, go out.
I should go out.
He said, look out.
They hang-- I don't know how many-- I don't remember how many.
They hang the Jew's head there.
You don't want to hang there.
I went to cafeteria.
No, I didn't wear a star.
But my face said.
In Sosnowiec, I stay mine girlfriend's friends overnight, and I left.
I couldn't wait.
And she had broken her wrist.
She was in Warsaw.
She was not in Sosnowiec.
This was her friends.
They tell me, look.
You have to wait maybe a couple weeks.
Said I couldn't do that.
Because I didn't know them.
I went to-- back to Cracow to the ghetto.
By train.
Yeah.
I say whatever will be will be.
I don't know.
Yes.
I came to Cracow to ghetto, to the ghetto.
And there was standing a friend, what used to live in our building.
And he said, you're going to sister?
I said, where are my sisters?
Rega was in Cracow.
So perfect.
I went to my sister.
